summary: McKenzie describes changing his website's link colors from a custom orange scheme back to the default blue to reduce visitor confusion and lower bounce rates. He notes that non-standard link colors, while aesthetically matching his site, may confuse users accustomed to the convention that blue underlined text is clickable. He also reports that his redesigned download buttons are performing well, with the 'Download Windows Free Trial' button generating the most clicks on his entire site according to CrazyEgg heatmap data.
key_points: Custom link colors that match a site's design can confuse users who expect the standard blue-underlined convention for clickable links
Bounce rate on the front page was higher than desired, motivating the change back to default link colors
Redesigned download buttons are performing well based on CrazyEgg heatmap data, with the free trial button being the hottest click target on the site
Small UI conventions like link color can have measurable impact on user behavior and conversion
money_quotes: I have been told that having them other colors has a tendency to confuse people who are used to the convention that anything blue and underlined can be clicked.
That Download Windows Free Trial button is, incidentally, the most incandescent place anywhere on my website.
key_insight: Respecting established web conventions like default link colors can reduce user confusion and improve click-through rates, even when custom colors look better aesthetically.
